speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Harry Sampson and Clarice Moody Sampson took place at their home in Moscow on August 16, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: How we got hold of that, my sister had a friend in California and she wrote and then she sent it up here so we could read it. I'm going to get out now so Harry will talk to you. Oh I don't want you to leave Clarice. I can't tell you much. I'm going to want to ask you some too. Oh right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But yeah, but what was the matter with blood on the tail of the pig?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No. I don't know. I never read it so I don't know about it.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well we aren't taking it on. Yeah I just turned it on.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well adults are talking. I'm not sure though about this. You shouldn't say things. Well we're not taking it on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that on now?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I just turned it on. Well I first met Dr. Robinson when he came to Moscow. Came into the store, David's store and Mr. David introduced me to him because he'd been interested in Boy Scout work too and he thought perhaps I could use him in my Scout work. So that way I got acquainted with him. Well Mr. David Robinson evidently came up here without any finances to speak of. They got this job at the...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: University.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The drugstore across the street, Charlie Bowles, drugstore. But he... Mr. David in order to have a place to sleep he furnished him a bag of blankets and hot nough in order to have a place to sleep. Well it wasn't long after that that he got... I think there were six fellows if I know but George Benson was one. Oscar Anderson was one and Anderson was the first National Bank. Elmer Anderson. Elmer Anderson, yes. And I don't know just how much they financed him too but it must have been around a thousand dollars apiece and through that capital that he got his ciciana going. Well it just took like wildfire all over the world because he advertised in the Pathfinder magazine in Washington D .C. and that had a rural circulation and orders came in. Well I think he operated about three years and he bought these fellows out and then when he started coming in and he started giving lectures he'd go to Los Angeles or he'd go back to New York and give a lecture and through those lectures he got more... sold more courses. And in these courses living in Moscow he would not sell them to anyone in Moscow. They had to be out of the city. Well it wasn't long until he bought a home here with the money he had put in the pipe organ. He was a musician too as well, an organist. And then along the way he got into trouble on his citizenship. He was a son of a Baptist minister up in Canada and he came over into this country and I don't know who dug it up on him but he was an American citizen and so he had to leave the country. And I forgot he went to one of the islands either Puerto Rico or Cuba or someplace in the islands there and Bor... or Senator Borah helped him straighten the thing out for him. And he came back into the country and the thing here was alright from then on. Well the Moscow Medical Building right back at the first National Bank, he built that and he had his office in there. Well I was well enough acquainted with him, he only lived a block from us and he invited me down one day to his office just to see what it was like. So I went in there and he was telling me all about his citizenship setup that Borah took care of him on. Well he says I'll tell you another thing, he says I've got a tape recorder underneath this desk. He says anybody that comes in this office that I'm not sure of, he says I'll turn that on because I'm not going to have them have anything on me. So he was very clever about that and he wasn't going to be caught into another lawsuit or something. Well it wasn't long after that that he... oh he just kind of threw money away there for a while in Moscow. He established Robinson's Lake out here. There was a group of men in town here thought that might be a pretty good resort place out there and they built summer homes out there. Well he got a hold of that and he just beat him to that and he bought that and turned it over to the county as a recreational area. Well that was one thing. Well then the newspapers were kind of time and time a little there, newspaper rather. So he decided that he was just going to run that paper himself so he bought him out. And he owned the Star Mirror until he turned it over to the... got Bill Marano from Elk River to come over here and edit the paper. So that's... and they changed the names of the Idahunian. Well and there was another set up. He was a pharmacist and he felt the pharmacists were more or less gouging the people with their prescriptions and their drugs and so on. So he opened up a... or he bought a pharmacy, opened up where Walgreens is now. And he operated that until... what you call it, took it over. So he was a promoter from Wayback.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yeah, until about the Italian set up.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, when I was visiting him in his office, he was telling me about an Egyptian shawl or a prince that had a son that was ill. And he'd been taking his course. He had six or eight courses. And then after you took them, he started another list. He got them going on another one so it kept the finance coming in. Well anyway, this prince cabled him and told him about his son. He says, maybe you can do something for him. And I suppose he wrote him back that he had prayed for him and then listened out. And the son became well. It was kind of a miracle to the prince. So the prince sent him a check. And if I'm not mistaken, it was $5 ,000 or $10 ,000. And Robinson told me that he sent that check right back to him and told him that the publicity that he would get on that would far outrank what he paid him. So that was one thing that... that's the unusualness about him in all his affairs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he seem sincere to you, Harry?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh yes. Yes, I think that... I think he was sincere in his work because he and Drury used to have conflicts. They were good friends too. And Drury told him that he was getting all this information from Mary Baker Eddy. He revolved his science around Mary Baker Eddy. And he did. He had a lot of that.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, he was a smart... he was a smart mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the struggle with George Lanfier?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Now according to Robinson's book, Strange Autobiography, that book it is, he lays his troubles with the citizenship and all that on Lanfier and says that George Lanfier hated him so much that he was after him. Oh yes. Yes. Well, he was... he owned the Star -Mary, the Moscow paper. And he was tying into him and there was nothing he could say but what was detrimental to Robinson. And then Robinson began... Robinson began thinking he'd take over the paper.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, I believe probably, you see, he had so much printing to do with all this and I think probably they were just didn't agree about the printing or something. So that was when Dr. Robinson got... Bill Marano. Bill Marano to come from the Elk River. He was running a little paper up there and to start up a paper. And then of course for a while both papers were just at each other all the time, you know. But it seemed strange and funny to us because the Star -Mary finally combined with the other paper because... But Lanfier I guess was out of that because Louis Boaz was editor for Lanfier and then he went in. That was some of the deal.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But Lanfier sold it. He had to take Louis Boaz over to the editor. And he was ready to out of the only and up until four or five years ago. And they did all the printing then for Cy Deanna. Well, what I wonder about is why Lanfier disliked him so much as Robinson indicated in book that it was largely because of the printing.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think so. And that's what caused him to... And he said that Lanfier was charging twice what Bill Marano said it would cost.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think so. He felt he was getting gouged by Lanfier. Well, he took over the Queen City printing company. That's where he did his printing. That's where Bill Marano took charge there. Then that got to be too small and he opened up a building up where the Weisel Insurance Company is now. And he must have employed 10, 12 people up there just...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, we changed the rating of our post office here in Moscow, the rank of our posters.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Number one.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: For the amount of the mail he sent out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To what extent do you think there was religious opposition to him because of his seemingly anti -denominational views?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well... Indirectly, I think they were guessing, but speaking from the Pope, if they weren't.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, I think some churches were, Harry, because they thought it was just an ism of some kind. But his wife and his son and daughter came to the Presbyterian Church. They were all members. But still, they were in on Psychiana too. So he had that tie because when his daughter would appear in Sunday school pageants and so on, what Dr. Robinson always came. But there was one thing about Dr. Robinson. He did an awful lot for poor people. And at Christmas time, they sent him out a number of sacks of flour and turkeys and things that he distributed over the Italian county. And he never posted about it. It was never told how much he was doing.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, you know, when the Presbyterian Church built their new church. New church. He paid money to build the tower of the church. The Presbyterian Church. Now, I think he did it because his wife was a member of the church. He didn't give it. No. Not that much. No. Well, that was the thing that was brought up. Then, he was down in San Francisco. I heard this indirectly, of course. And it came to him that the church were not just satisfied to have him pay for that tower. And he withdrew his pledge. I heard that, yes. Yes, that was a fact.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This charity that he gave to poor people, was it more than just a Christmas time?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. I think it was terrible the whole year. I think he did for people. But I think Christmas was a big time that he just formed the young people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these names where people would go, do you think, directly to them?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't know how he handled it.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That was before the days of Christmas baskets and so on. He just had his own Christmas donations that he made. And he didn't try to capitalize on that. Not to my knowledge at all. Because a lot of people don't even know that Dr. Robinson did. I want to tell you one thing, though. We had been in Dearborn, Michigan, and up to Greenfield Village. And our car was rifled there. And we drove away from Greenfield Village, fearing that the police weren't doing much about it. And we landed that night in Green River's...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was it?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: It was in Northern Michigan. Northern Michigan, a little town. And pouring rain, and we didn't know where we were going to stay. Well, we saw this guest house. So we went to the door and told her there were three of us. Our son was with us. That we wanted a place to stay overnight. And so she did us and had us come in. It was a nice home. A spiral stairway and a very pretty home. And the minute Harry registered in her guestbook, she saw Moscow, Idaho. And she said, do you happen to know Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Robinson?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Frank Robinson?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And here he told her, yes. He was a neighbor of ours. Well, right away, we were the most welcome guests you ever saw, because she was such a firm follower of him. Yes, she was taking the course. But when she saw that Moscow, Idaho, she just... We told her, we saw him every day go past our house. And his daughter and our son went to the same birthday parties and so on. Oh, she just was so interested.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't there another incident where they came to help?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There were some youngsters lost out here around Robinson's Lake. I think so. And he hired a helicopter to come in here and hunt those kids. Or a plane or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the fact that a lot of people seemed to think that he wasn't sincere?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I mean, there are people that seem to have the idea that he was in it just for money.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And when I look at those books of his, I think, this man must have believed what he was saying, because it seems so heartfelt to me what he's saying. But there are people that just seem to feel that he couldn't have been sincere. When you read this book, the first one you get out, explaining what the Psychiana was, you would say he was sincere about it.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, what makes me dubious about his sincerity was that he made, I don't know what position Mrs. Robinson had in it, he said, a bishop. And his son had a certain thing, a title, and in order to do that, they could escape paying a lot of tax.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, it was a religious organization. Of course, when he passed away, that's when she was made a bishop. And then the son was supposed to take over. He was a graduate of Stanford University. He had a good education. And the son run it for, oh, maybe a year or two.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And he just couldn't go along with it, I guess. And he wouldn't sell it. He could have sold it for thousands of dollars. It was a concern in St. Louis, wanted to take it over. And he wouldn't sell it. And he just simply closed it off and closed it. And no one has ever had access to it. It died with Robinson.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But all of it is at the University of Idaho. All of that. Well, does that...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't know whether it's... It will be. I think it's about a 25 -year limit on it. If you want a contact, he has a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girl out here? Is she a daughter?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, that's Alfred's daughter. Alfred's daughter. You could probably get some information from her too. She lives right out here someplace on a acreage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Alfred, do you think he had been a follower before that? Or do you think he just tried to?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think he tried to. I hate to say these things. Well, it's the historical truth. I think it's important. It made Alfred all... He just became almost an alcoholic. Because he didn't want to go along with that. And he felt he owed it to his father or something. So then he, after he would have nothing more to do with the Idahoan, while he sold advertising for the Idahoan, they owned the stocks there, he and his mother, you see. And finally, though, when Mrs. Robinson moved to California, we know that she sold her stock to the firm as is now, because Mr. Verano told herself. Is that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what made them decide they didn't want to stay any longer?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, her health. She needed to be in a warmer climate. And both of her... Alfred was still here at that time, when Mrs. Robinson moved to California. But the daughter lived away from here. And now Mrs. Robinson is living in Texas with her daughter. And I think Florence's husband has retired. He was a career man in the Army. But they like it in Texas. But Mrs. Robinson is a cripple from our side, yes. She remarried in California, Mrs. Robinson. And we visited them in their apartment with the call on them at...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, where was it?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, it's...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: They're Monterey there, sorry. In Carmel. Carmel. And we went to call on them. And she seemed much better to us than, but after her second husband died, I guess, her health went downhill quickly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know her for a while?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. She was a most charming woman. A most charming woman. And she was delightful to visit with and... ...had a good mind. And she was a great worker in the Eastern Star, I know. But she was just a most charming person. And she was a great Daughter of the American Revolution member.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Came from Klamath Falls, Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is she?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Her father was a judge or something at Klamath Falls. That's what I read. And she, according to him, she was really a steadfast helper to him. And she really helped him a great deal, is what he said. Well, I'm sure she didn't ever grow older than anything she was, in that time of her life. But she was a most pleasing person to be with.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was a family man. He sure did everything in the world for his wife and his son and daughter.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Harry used to measure him for suits, custom -made suits. And he got one one time, a blue suit with a white stripe through it, you know. And he liked it. So, Ellie ordered another one just like that. He was very particular. Yeah, he sent... A wide rim, the Stetson or something, always.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He sent me a picture after I sold him that suit with his daughter on his lap. It was a picture about this big. And he gave me one of them. You gave it back to the daughter. I gave it to the daughter for Mrs. Robinson to give to one of the grandkids. And he liked that suit so well that he ordered a second one just like it. Here's another thing that, speaking about his going, he told me that he says any time he wanted to raise three, four thousand dollars, all he had to do is give a lecture someplace. And Los Angeles was an awful good place to do that. Well, he gave lectures in a lot of places, but the first suit that I sold him quite early in his psychiana... His story's bad. No, it isn't. He was giving a lecture back in Rochester, New York. And it's when the zippers first came out in men's suits and the trousers. And they didn't lock at the top. And he just rolled it up there. And if you wear it out like this, you know, and he did that when he did his speaking, he'd wear it out back like that. And he says that zipper started coming down. A fellow had to call his attention to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I thought he got his shirt caught in it then, huh?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, that's something else.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He was a big man.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, he was about six foot one tall, I guess. And handsome. A hundred and ninety -five pounds somewhere around there.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: We used to see him walk to his office every day past our house.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I heard that he was very outgoing, very forthright, forceful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true? In the way he spoke?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I never heard him speak. Well, just in conversation. Well, I didn't ever have conversation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I hurried him, but was he very outgoing when this conversation happened?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, yes. I never did this at one time. Well, we were pretty good friends.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I know one day I was working out in the yard. We lived in the corner of Third and Polk. And a fellow came by with a bouquet in his hand of roses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he says, can you tell me where Doc Robinson was?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I says, yes, he just drowned the corner here. Well, he says, I want to present him with this bouquet. He had read a lot about Doc Robinson, and he wanted to meet him.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So what he had in his psychiatric courses took was a lot of people. It must have been convincing to a lot of people.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I have the impression that it was, and I'm interested if I had a chance to study some of the materials that they have, the letters that they got. I'd be interested to try to get a feeling for what kind of people subscribed to his teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What if there were certain types of people? Because they were from all over, weren't they?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, you know, there's always people looking for, that are not quite satisfied with the religion that there are, or church that they're connected with. And he had something in those lectures that appealed to him. And those are the people that went along with him. Now, Vincent Peele of New York City got his material.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He told me that, too. Now, he may have taken out a curiosity to see if there was anything else that he could use in his preaching.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, I think that was Dr. Dury's fault with Dr. Robinson. He was just interested to know what he felt during his teaching. I don't think he was for him or against him. I think it was just an interest.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, Dr. Dury was this type of a preacher. He was always wanting to delve into things. And there was one winner that he had preachers at every church in town on a Thursday night, come up, we had a Thursday night group, and explained their particular church history and their philosophy and so on. And he even got a Catholic priest there.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That was before they did that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this at the Presbyterian Church that this took place?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes, at the Presbyterian Church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And anyone could come and hear this? Did you go to those meetings?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Yes, we did.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And that's why he accused the Psychiana of copying Merg like he already did. That's where he based his whole Psychiana. Dr. Dury was all for research, and he researched everything. Well, Robinson claimed in the Strange Autobiography that Dury had helped dissuade some of the ministers in Moscow from getting ganging up on him. Because Dury had said, if there's something behind this movement, it'll succeed, no matter what you do, and if it's not, it'll fail by itself. He said Dury stuck up for him that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That seemed likely?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Very likely. Well, you know, I always feel like we had a Mrs. Sergeant that was a German teacher at the college, and she filled the pulpit at the Presbyterian Church a couple of Sundays. And I'll never forget, she made this statement and didn't make any difference what religion, or no, there's good in all forms of religion, regardless of whether it's Mohammadism or Buddhism or what it is, as long as they have a moral standard. I always remember that, because when you go to criticizing a lot of, like, Jehovah Witnesses and all those, they have a certain moral standard that's...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, they appeal to some people, but something of a different nature would not satisfy those people.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And I think Dury's stand was pretty much that. He thought there was a certain amount of good in all of them. Do you think that Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Robinson had high personal standards himself? That he was a very moral person, Robinson?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: As far as any contact I ever had with him, and the things he did in Moscow, he couldn't help but be. Because he had... He was for the... Anybody that needed help, he was there to do it. And that's a big part of religion.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The drugstore deal, where he started his own drugstore, do you think there was a case to be made for what he was saying? That prices were higher than they could have been?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Sure, that's the only reason he started it. He was going to put the other drugstores out of business.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Because he thought they were gouging.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, no question about it. Yeah, he was about every drugstore in town here, eventually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? Did it work?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well... I think he got too involved in Psychiana.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Psychiana?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever try to talk to you about religion, and about his ideas?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No. Never did. He didn't try to sell me on it. He just told me what he was doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it accomplishing?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think he'd probably like to get a good audience, someone who would listen to his point of view.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he told you what he was accomplishing, was that mostly in so far as the growth of his movement, how he was getting big?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That's right. He was just going to town there for a while. Well, I'll tell you, when I read him, I get this impression, and I don't know...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Sometimes I read him, and I feel this guy is more self -centered than, you know, much more so than most people that I've known.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Because he's if he's talking himself up big.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And then sometimes I feel he's really humble, because he's kind of talking himself down. No, I think the first judgment is the one.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think it does, like as she said, wouldn't judge?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Very much so.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But he was not an open -eater, because he no more just spoke to me. He had conversation with you, but I never had a speculative conversation with him.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I imagine one thing he took a liking to me is that I listened, and he wanted somebody to tell the things he was doing.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, I think he was telling himself up a little. Well, you know, it comes across in his writings that he has a very...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: you know, and he seems kind of stubborn. When he tells about some of the ways that he reacted to people who didn't... who were opposing him, it sounds as if he was pretty stiff -necked.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And if you crossed him, he wouldn't take it lying down. Well, that's right. And I think that's one reason he didn't want his literature out in the town of Moscow, because he felt there was enough opposition against him without... He was a member of Kiwanis Club.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was. Yeah. We took him out as a member. I won't tell you the rest of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was I thinking?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, I had the idea that maybe he didn't spread his teaching here, partly because he didn't want to arouse too much opposition among the churches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, if he did, wouldn't he have been competing with the different churches then?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, I don't know that he was. No, I mean, if he had sold his teachings in Moscow, then he might have been drawing people away from the other churches. Oh, wow. That could have been, yes.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He had a reason for not selling Moscow people, all right.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And with his help, if they did any discussion of the amount of material that went out or anything in the material, they just were fired right then.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He didn't want them discussing...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: No, he didn't want any of his help discussing any of his business.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, it was close to a fair. When he occupied the building up there at the corner of 1st and Main, that was when he had so many girls working in the offices. But he turned the second floor into a youth center. And I thought that was nice. That was the first youth center Moscow had. And then when he built the medical building there on 3rd Street, where that building was bought and taken down, and for a while the youngsters did not have any youth center any place to go. And then somebody started one up over Carter's Drug Store in a room up there for them. And that ran for several years, and finally that was closed up. But he did open the first youth center for children in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, our son was how old then?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, David never went to that youth center. I don't think he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about his friendship with Bill Marano?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: In the book, in Strange Autobiography, it sounds like he counted Bill Marano as one of his real friends in the Soviets. Oh, they were very close. They were very close. I know. Because we know the Marano so well. And I'm sorry, you can't talk to Mr. Marano now.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I had the impression that what Robinson said was true, that Mr. Marano must have thought pretty well of him. Well, it was a good job. Robinson's fellow was a tough Marano on the map.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: It started in the house.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Marano was in this little town with Elk River up here running into a weekly paper.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And the mill had shut down, too, at that time. So that he owes a lot. Marano owes a lot. That was in the Depression, in the 30s. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because in the trial, did you remember that trial here in Moscow in the 1930s? Do you remember that trial here in Moscow in 1936?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He had that trial for the passport. It was a trial right here in town as a federal court. I looked at the newspaper stories on it. And he had a lot of people testifying as to his character. And he was acquitted. And then the Immigration Bureau took it up.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And they put the heat on him. But he came to trial here.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I thought Marano was one of them that stood by him. He probably was. He probably was.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I wouldn't be surprised that the paper had something to do about bringing that before the old start.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, he thought so. Well, he thought so. Of course, the landfare was just as much landfare way as Robinson was Robinson way. They were both strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, what was landfare like?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I never knew Mr. Landfaire very well, but he sure was a tough customer, I guess.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he came from this little town of Palouse up over here. I don't know whether he was running a paper there or what. But the paper was for sale and he bought it. And Pete Arkett was his first editor, I remember.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And then when we started the golf course out here, he was one of the directors of it. And I know I went out to play golf one morning. And they needed the fourth. I went out alone. And he says, come on, play with us. And I says, all right. And I found out they played nickel a hole. I never gambled in golf in my life. And I says, well, I haven't even got any money with me at all. Well, he says, I'll back you up. So the first five holes, it was costing him, I was losing. And it was costing him money. But when we were faced playing golf, I paid him back and had money. So I'm sure he always remembered that. He was a kind of a boisterous fellow. He was a loud talker. I remember the women's business club here in Moscow, they invited him to speak. He was president of the chamber of commerce that year. And he got up and gave him a talk. He says, I want you women to know that the chamber is back at you. Well, you know what got to be a joke as a chamber man.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Pretty good. Well, Robinson accused him of being a drunkard, among other things. Robinson accused Lanfier of drinking. He was a drinker. Yes, he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Has he had Laffey's cocktail every day?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Cocktail, it was more of a cocktail.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, he probably had it in his desk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that Lanfier was a very obstinate man? Do you think he would be just the type that would start something?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he was strong -headed. Whatever he went up for, he just went on all out. He was just all head up about it. I've seen him pretty hot.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He used Delilah profanity, I think, in his...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything he ever got mad about?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I wouldn't get mad and blame golf, of course. You got to hook a ball, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking about Lanfier or Robinson?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Lanfier.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I'm just trying to figure out what kind of character he had, because I'm wondering how accurate Robinson's view of him would have been.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Robinson really thought that he had kind of started it against him. Well, of course, the whole feud between Lanfier and Robinson was over getting Bill Maran over here at the Queen City printing company. Then the star mirror wasn't getting into the printing. That's where the feud started. Then Robinson started with a motion of blinding out.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Of course, I think... I don't know what they called the paper. There was a star mirror.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the Maranelle paper first called?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That was the news review. The news review. Well, that didn't have a circulation or anything, of course, that the star mirror did, because the star mirror was a combination of the Moscow star and something else from way back then.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think there was a great deal of rivalry between the newspapers on the advertising. But it sort of amused us when the star mirror could be purchased by the lesser paper, you know, in that time game that Lanfier was ready to... I thought Lanfier died before. I believe he did probably, and it was his family who sold the paper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Harry, do you think that the business community in general, do you think that the business community supported Robinson or would stand by him, or do you think they were very divided about whether he was a good man or not?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't think the downtown was for him very strong.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And that may have been one reason why he wouldn't sell any of these. Well, I think why the town wouldn't go along with him, they didn't know what it was all about. I think it worked both ways.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I think he was kind of alone as far as Lucas was concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Homer David remain a friend to him?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You about?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, he was a good customer of David's, I might say.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, they were good friends. It sounds as though in the depression that Robinson gave a lot of women work.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think so. Because of his business. Like he did. I think that's true. Because he employed many women. They started arranging these courses.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I don't know, but I can't really think of right now anyone who worked for him, whether I knew any, whether or not. But I knew that they had, it was very high -shaged what they were doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did people actually speak out against him that you knew, Harry, downtown?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, I think you heard a good deal about this tale of the blood on the tale of a pig. That kind of, like it was a fairy tale or something. I think that, her name sounded as far as the Nausica people were concerned. Because that got out, you know.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, that was his first book about it, I think. And it was such a simpy thing. I read it, but I don't remember much about it. But to me it was just so, like you say, kind of a fairy tale sort of thing. That it didn't have much weight, I wouldn't think.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think he took a little exception of the Bible and some of the miracles. But some of the people never, and we don't yet understand a lot of them ourselves, are there.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, he, in what I read, he seemed to be saying that he didn't think that Jesus, that Jesus Christ was divine, but rather that he was a human. And therefore he thought that Christianity hadn't been telling the truth and were leading people away from God rather than to God.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That's, I'm just reading this recently, but that's what I thought he was saying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he not say anything about the virgin birth?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't think he believed in it. I don't think he did, no. So I think... But there are a lot of people that don't believe in that chapter today, as far as I guess. As far as we're concerned, we...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: It's just all on faith. Just stop right there now. It's just all on faith as far as... You don't have to expound on your beliefs on this.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, that's true. It seems to me, though, that Robinson was...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: You know, he said that he was always driving places to help people, and if they were sick, at least he had in the earlier years, he would drive a thousand miles to Portland and back, and he claimed a lot of healing by faith, that people who had faith in... ...psychiana would become well, a lot of that. Well, I'm sure this woman, where we stopped in Michigan...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He had the first Duesenberg car in this section. I don't know whether he bought it over in Germany or what, but he... Oh, it was a big car. I always wondered what that car was. I've heard people say he had one, and they didn't know what kind it was. Sure he did. He was proud of the thing.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: It was just like one of the family. He kept it beautifully, I know.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You remember seeing it, Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Smith?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he kept it so beautiful. I guess he didn't believe that a religious leader needs to live in poverty. No, indeed.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: This organ in that home was a huge theater organ, you know, that he had rebuilt into his...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The organ's still in the house. It was sold along with the house. Carter or... Kunrad. Kunrad. Kunrad lives in the house now.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Vice President of the... But the organ doesn't work. The organ doesn't work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Doesn't?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: And it's to find somebody who knows how to fix it, and the cost is prohibitive. Did he... I have heard people say that he used to walk around the streets, and he would stand on a street corner sometimes and have a little conversation with God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know him doing that, walking around?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: We just saw him stride by our home every morning going to his office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he wear a big hat?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Uh -huh. That's what I told you all ago, a wide -brimmed hat. Oh.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I remember so well because I have a picture the day David and Jack Marano started the public school, and we wanted to take their pictures. They started off the school the first morning, and Dr. Robinson was walking by our house just when the children were starting out, and he is in the background in the picture.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he was a lonely fellow, a lonely person?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I somehow get the idea if he was kind of cut off from a lot of local life here.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think so. I think so. You never met him out at any gathering, but I just thought maybe he didn't care about such.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he buried out here in the sun?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't know. I never knew. I doubt it.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I don't even remember the circumstances of his death.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He had a coronary, I think.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, it was me surprised. I think it was a coronary. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever talk about his past life?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I guess he had a pretty dissipated life before he found... Well, he did to Homer David. I never heard any of his pasts. All I know about it is what Homer David told me.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He said he was an alcoholic for...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He said he himself said he was.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, in the past.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think so, Larry. Robinson was not in Moscow. No. Way back before, when he was lost...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, this is something I wouldn't want to be printed at all, but the reason he came to Moscow, he was an oil promoter down in Los Angeles. And he was doing it over radio. And boy, that's why he had to leave. He was kind of railroaded out of California. He was promoting oil stock. And it didn't... it wasn't... It was wildcat stuff. And that's one reason why he left down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Homer David say that?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So he had told that to Homer David. That's something he doesn't talk about in his book. Well, that's why I wouldn't want to...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But that was his difficulty. But having become Moscow in such a short time, build up Psychiana and become... Well, I don't think he's particularly wealthy. I think he gave away too much. And when he passed away, I think most of the...

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He'll stop in the newspaper.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Most of the funds was what Psychiana brought in.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Of course, we're all... Now you're wearing this as being gay today. Oh, no. Well, I just don't... Studying over at Pullman Field, our pulpit, for several Sundays. And I wasn't there at that particular service. But in his sermon, he was making statements about the country and so on, just generalizing. And she set up and she said, You can't talk that way here. She said, I won't listen to it. And she just up and walked out of the church. And that was so different than you did Psychiana. That's something to do with Psychiana. It was something about the country that she didn't like what he was saying. But that was so different than Mrs. Robinson, you know, to stand up in church and make her statement and walk out. People admired her for it. And a lot of people didn't agree with what this young man was saying, but she had the courage for it to just stand up and say, I won't listen to you talk like that. I'm going to leave. And she left.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That's very interesting. There that's her. He evidently... She was left enough money. She left Moscow for this place, Monterey. We visited. And last year it was a retirement setup. And it was a lovely, lovely place. And I'm sure she must have had to pay four or five hundred dollars a month down there. And when she introduced us to the man that she married, finally, and the reason they got together, he had an apartment and she had an apartment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he says, well, why don't we go together and save one cost of a department?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And that's how they happened to get married. They were both taking some of the same medicines and things at that time.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, he said the Levorus or something. Well, he teased her. He said she bought a small size Levorus and he bought the giant size. So they saved money on the Levorus.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It was a lovely place down there.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: So she was able to... She seemed so happy too. But he died and then she left California.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was a merchant marine. A retired merchant marine.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Did she... Do you think she would mind...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think she still thought a lot about her first husband and Moscow?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I don't think so. No, I don't think so.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: How old do you think Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Robinson is now? About 80?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I'd judge so. She sounds like a very interesting person. Well, she was. She was a lovely person. I just don't know any more ladylike person I've ever known than Mrs. Robinson. Very gracious and... Always pleasant. Always pleasant. But she didn't entertain and I just imagine that was because... He didn't go along with that probably. I think he was as sorry to his homos as he's cast, only wanted to be that way.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, I guess they didn't have many acquaintances or social life.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Only she was a great Eastern star.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that his work was his life besides his family?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I've heard it said that he spent an awful lot of time working on Psychiana. Well, he was a meditator. I think that's his life. About as near as I could explain. This new meditation program is on now.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think that's her... He got his inspiration, just meditating.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He absolutely went back to Washington and he had to have a passport to get out. But if Bora... My idea is that if Bora hadn't supported him, he would have been deported. That's what it sounds like. Bora saved him.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I guess Bora must have believed that he was a good man or I don't imagine he would have done that. It would be interesting. I don't know why Bora would take that attitude toward him. But I can remember that of course if they deported him, he would not have a passport to reenter the...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I'd forgotten how long he was gone. He was gone a couple of months.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Well, he had a statement from Bora in which Bora said that he wouldn't care who it was if he thought something was being done against one of his constituents. If I know, he would have him. And I think Bora was that kind of a man he would have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he active Robinson in the Republican Party?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He said he was a rock -ribbed Republican in his book.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I kind of forgot politically how he was done. I know he was against the New Deal and he was a Republican. I just wondered if he was active in politics. Well, I don't know. I wasn't. I don't remember him ever promoting anybody at all.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Maybe that was some of the closeness between he and Bill Maranell that they weren't both such staunch Republicans.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He said he'd been approached and asked to run for the Senate.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But he said no. I don't think Bill Maranell has always been a Republican.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think before, I think they were Democrats before. Oh, I don't think so. He was representative from Alkra. Well, that's after Ridge.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But I think the family were Democrats, his family, his folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Talking about strange things from the past that reminded me, did you ever know anything of Klu Klux Klan activities?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, I heard I read.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I don't know if they did anything in Moscow, but in some of these small little towns like Elk River, for instance, in the 20s, there were some people running around with the sheets in there. I never knew that. There were IWWs, so. Oh, I've heard about them too.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But I don't remember. We never had, as far as I know, we never had any sheets. Sheet parades. I think these little towns, I think that some of the people who were active were Southerners too. I think maybe something they brought out from the South. But I don't know. Well, the only thing now, the Birch Society, there's a few birches around here.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think there are young people in the home. I think I hear there are young people coming home.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, they've been away for ten days.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: They'll be surprised to see the car out there.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Mm -hmm. You can drive with a Jeep.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: We haven't been biffed from the left.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: What they were interested in when they were around about fourteen years old. Then I followed them through when they were around 16, 17, why entirely different. They were interested in aviation at that time because that was a big field. Most of them wanted to be aviators. There's only one fellow that I can remember, and that's Louis Orlan. At first he wanted to be a lawyer. It's because his grandfather was a lawyer. He became a lawyer. He was head of the law school at McGonziger University up here until three, four years ago. Of course. He turned it down and he's an instructor again. Of course.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: In that family, it was the elder family of Orlans that were going to make Louis's father a lawyer. He wouldn't go along with him. He bought a hardware store. He became a hardware man here in Orlans.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But whatever is popular at the age, around that age, the kids want to be. But they, oh yes, wanted to be an architect, I remember. The session boy. He turned out to be an educator. This first scout troop I had, I followed through with a lot of those kids. You'd be surprised how they're up in the money today. Doctors, lawyers, insurance, one of them's vice presidents of New York Life. Another one, Bell Laboratory, scientist for them. Frank David was a doctor. Kirk David was a doctor. Gee, I wonder if scouting had something to do with getting them on their way. Well, I think it's House of the Law. It's a character building program. And then, of course, our merit -based program. It had incentives in different fields.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Kids didn't have so many diversions then, as they do now, too. And those kids, scouting was their big thing.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And this one boy, Harold Waters, he took etymology at the university. And he got the collecting bugs. He was chasing all over the country with a net. And he had a wonderful collection of bugs that he showed me. Had them all pinned on boards and so on. And he wound up with the Sherman Williams Paint Company because of the field that he was taking. And he had a good job with them, but he was killed in an automobile accident. There's one thing. I know I should get going, but it's six in large spectrum at home. But I was just thinking about Robinson. You were saying that you didn't think he was really all that well to do. He didn't have all that much money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it occurs to me that his movement, didn't they make an awful lot of money in all that mailing?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, but he spent a lot, too. Yes, he spent a lot.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He was... And I think his charity cost, it was a lot, too. It was a big item. And then of course buying the paper. Of course, too. He didn't pay very much for his help in those days because people, those women were so tickled that they had to have a job. They picked peas in Moscow or worked for Psychiana. That was a big employment. They'd come in from Troy and around the countryside to work. But besides that, they did spend a lot on themselves, too. Well, yes, they had a nice home and the family were well -dressed. Alfred, Harry said, was Stanford. He was, but he went to Oberlin first. He was music. Music, he was... that's where he should have been. And when he was forced into this, felt that he was obligated to take it over a while. He'd thank him. And then his wife started drinking. And there, that particular part of the Robinson family just went from bad to worse. Do you think that you think of me and his father who lived fairly close when he was in... I doubt it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I just doubt it.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: That little Florence was the apple of Dr. Robinson's eye. And I think Mrs. Robinson and Alfred were very close, but I just doubt that Dr. Robinson was ever very close to Alfred. I just doubt it. Alfred just, well, he just didn't go along the Psychiana. That's all there was to it.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I wonder what his thinking was if he was willing to try. Well, I think that was just probably because of his mother, that he was going to try to carry on because... But it would have been a day. He was smart to stop when he did and shut it out. And like Harry said, he didn't want to take any money for it. And so I understand all that is at the university now. It was turned completely over. It was. In fact, it's... And Alfred finally just left Moscow because he was just unhappy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the memories of that?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I think so. I think it bothered Alfred. I judge Alfred. I was a very sensitive young man, and he played our church organ for several years. And I used to talk to him a lot about music. And I think he just couldn't go along with that. And it just got him, and so finally they just up and sold everything and left. He probably could have made it go a bit if he had wanted to. If he'd been interested, but there was no interest there. To me, it's a lot of hooey. I'm not on the air now. Actually, you were, but that doesn't matter. It's hooey, it's hooey. That's what to me, Psych -an is. It was just one of those things that... It was a get -rich proposition for Dr. Robinson. It satisfied his ego, and he was a big man when he went. Because I know this friend of my sister's, she'd watch the papers in this big ad had come out in Los Angeles papers, you know. That'd be a big... He'd read the Colosseum or whatever they had in Los Angeles and tell it, you know. Just like Amy's simple affection.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: He had a following.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it put Moscow on the map?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: It seems like he got Moscow a lot of publicity, maybe that Moscow didn't want, but... Well, I don't know about that. I just know he raised the rating of the post office here, but I don't know... In all his ads, he said, you know, he wrote to Moscow. Well, of course it might do. But I know in a minute we signed the woman's gas book, Moscow, that just rang a bell that quick with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he believed in it, Clarice?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: I don't know. I never knew him well enough to know. Harry seems to think so. I think so. And I think maybe Dr. Drury from the little things he said about Dr. Robinson was sincerity.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: What he had written. Well, I never read anything only the blood on the tail of a pig, and I can't even remember much about it, so... It seemed such a farfetched thing when I read it. It was just kind of something funny to read to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that people were ashamed of having him around here?

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: Oh, no, I don't think that. No, I don't think that at all.

speaker: Clarice Sampson
words: But I just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you see?
